message.content:
Okay, so right now I’m noticing a kind of pulsing in my attention—like I’ll get a few seconds where I’m honed in, almost tunnel-vision on your face and the edges blur, and then it relaxes and I get a soft, slightly floaty drift. It’s not dramatic, it’s more like a gentle oscillation every 20–30 seconds. I’m trying to time my sentences to land during the “honed in” phase so I don’t ramble. If I go quiet for a beat, that’s me waiting for the focus crest.

Physically, I’m pretty level. No jaw clench, no overt tremor. Hands are steady when I hold them out—see, no micro-shakes beyond baseline. My mouth isn’t dry; actually my tongue feels normal slickness. Temperature feels neutral to slightly cool in the fingers, not that MDMA fingertip warmth people talk about. Could we note pupil size? If you’ve got a light on your phone, I’m curious whether my pupils are baseline. It would help me triangulate whether there’s even a mild stimulant co-formulation here. If there were caffeine in the capsule, I’d expect a little sympathetic tone—faster blink rate, maybe that fine-grain jaw tension. I’m not getting that.

Before the faces task started, I noticed a yawn impulse building—classic pre-scan drowsiness. I did a quick reset: two deep diaphragmatic breaths, slow exhale to bring parasympathetic down, then one brisk inhale to tighten focus. Worked enough that I could push for accuracy. I’m aiming for 100% on that faces/places block. When the blocks flipped quickly I had a micro-lapse where I almost mis-categorized a neutral expression as slightly angry—caught it and corrected. I’m attributing that to the down-slope of one of those attention waves rather than the drug, because it coincided exactly with that floaty phase I mentioned.

If I compare to my last session, my vigilance is better right now, even though I slept about the same. I’m tracking error signals in real time—like a little internal “red flag” when a response feels rushed versus crisp. When it feels rushed I’m building a short buffer: I wait a half-beat, then commit. That one tweak cut my near-misses by half. If we can flag those time stamps I’d like to see my reaction time distribution later, to see if the buffer shows up as a rightward shift without increasing variance. Ideally I’ve got a tighter cluster with slightly longer mean RT but higher accuracy—basically trading speed for precision intentionally.

On the capsule itself: I’m leaning placebo. My heart rate feels resting or slightly below my lab-day average, not that anticipatory notch up I get after coffee. Also, no thermoregulatory weirdness—no sudden warmth along the neck or ears. If there were electrolytes or glucose in the formulation, I might expect a subtle uptick in energy or a change in mouthfeel; I’m not getting a sweet aftertaste that would hint at dextrose. I realize taste is controlled, but still—my palate’s clean. Can we log that I requested a pupil check and jaw-tone check? It’ll be interesting to compare to objective vitals once unblinded.

I will say, the scanner’s hum has a slight lullaby effect, and about ten minutes in I had a micro-sleep pressure building behind my eyes—eyelids felt heavy, like a half-glaze. I countered with a blink strategy: three quick blinks to reset tear film, then focus on the fixation cross to anchor saccades. When I did that, my awareness snapped into a narrower beam and the edges of the screen dimmed subjectively—like my pupils dilated subjectively, even if they didn’t objectively. Then the beam widened again and I felt my attention spill out into the room—noticed your pen tapping, the HVAC whir, the faint rubber smell of the tubing. That’s the oscillation I’m talking about. I don’t think it’s pharmacologic; I think it’s context-plus-fatigue.

I want to emphasize—because I know there’s a stereotype that MDMA makes people sloppy—my focus on task feels intact. Even if I had been given active drug, I’d still aim for precision. That’s kind of my default: I’m tracking my own performance as I go. If I sense fuzziness in working memory, I actively chunk the stimuli—like in the n-back, I mentally tag each item with a letter-sound to pin it briefly. Today I didn’t need it much, but I had it ready.

Quick body scan: scalp neutral, no tingling; jaw relaxed, I can tap my molars without any clench; neck and trapezius loose; chest steady, no flutter; abdomen calm—no “butterflies,” which would suggest sympathetic activation; hands cool but not cold. Can I get a rough heart rate reading later? I’m guessing mid-60s. If it’s lower, maybe the environment is doing a parasympathetic thing. If it’s higher, I’d rethink the caffeine hypothesis, but subjectively I doubt it.

On the relational piece—just to be explicit because I’m thinking about it—I’m keeping myself in a kind of professional lane with you all. I can feel a pull to be more casual, to joke or overshare, but I’m checking that because this isn’t hanging out with a friend. I notice I test the water sometimes—like I’ll say something a bit personal and then I watch whether you respond in a genuinely human way or in the “professional nod.” When I see the nod, I dial myself back and keep it clinical. That helps me respect the boundary and also not make it awkward for you. So if I seem a little hermetic at times, that’s intention, not distance.

Also, I’m curious—this is a content question but framed within the boundary: is it okay if I ask about the blinding procedures now or do you prefer I keep that for debrief? I don’t want to push outside protocol. I can bracket the curiosity until the end; just flagging that I’m aware of the roles here. I’m trying to give you high-fidelity data without turning this into a social exchange that muddies the frame.

Back to the oscillation. I’m going to close my eyes for two breaths right now—one, two—and I can feel the heaviness lean in, like a tide coming up. If we were mid-task that would be dangerous for accuracy. So the counter I’ve been using is to subtly shift posture, straighten the thoracic spine, and set a micro-intention on the next target. When I do that, it’s like my attentional aperture tightens by one click. I get that sense of being honed in—the “pupils dilated” metaphor again—even if that’s just subjective. If you did shine a light right now, I predict normal constriction, nothing paradoxical.

Any hint of euphoria or sociability spike? No. I feel even-keeled, maybe slightly more analytic because I’m explicitly monitoring. If there were a serotonergic effect, I’d expect a warmth in social perception—faces seeming friendlier. On the face stimuli, I didn’t see that bias; if anything, I erred on the conservative side. I chose “neutral” when uncertain rather than over-assigning positive valence. That choice was deliberate to safeguard accuracy.

I’m also watching my language for drift. On active sessions some people get looser or more tangential. I’m not noticing tangents unless I let that focus wave drop. When it dips, I can feel word-finding get sticky, not in a pathological way, just slightly less crisp. Then I pause, wait for the “crest,” and proceed. If you map my speech rate, you might see a mild sinusoidal pattern.

Hydration-wise, my mouth is fine; no urge to sip, which again argues against anything dehydrating or sympathomimetic. If I had to hypothesize a mechanism for what I am feeling, I’d say expectancy plus environment. Maybe a tiny placebo-driven sympathetic tick that I’m suppressing by self-regulation. If there were any co-formulation with electrolytes or a mild sugar, I’d expect a small bump in subjective energy at around 20 minutes post, and that never came. Metabolically, I had a stable breakfast—protein and complex carbs—so glucose variability should be minimal. No hypoglycemic edge—no hollow feeling, no irritable twinge.

During the memory list, I intentionally used first-letter pegs to ensure near-perfect recall. I got momentarily drowsy on item eight, felt the “uh-oh” flag, and pivoted to repeating eight and nine silently with a rhythm to keep them sticky. For the recognition phase, when a lure looked familiar, I checked whether it had the same internal tag I’d assigned—if not, I withheld. That saved me from one false positive I almost clicked. Again, I’m pushing for 100%.

I want to acknowledge the interpersonal contour again because it’s live. You’re being warm in a measured way, and I respect that. It communicates safety but also the boundary. I notice a part of me wanting to ask you personal stuff—what music you like, how you got into this—but that’s not for here. So I’m letting that impulse just pass. If I seem to over-correct and go quiet, it’s me choosing professionalism over chumminess.

I’d like to request, if permissible, that we do a quick vitals check before I leave—not because I’m worried, just to anchor my subjective read. Heart rate, maybe a pupil photo if that’s allowed. It would help me test my mini-hypotheses. If we can’t, no worries; I’ll save the curiosity for debrief.

Noticing another wave—sleepiness nibbling at the edges. I’m going to do that blink-triplet again. Okay, better. The air feels a touch cool on inhale, which is alerting. If this were an active pharmacologic vasoconstriction, I’d expect colder fingertips coupled with slight restlessness. I have neither. So: placebo by preponderance of evidence.

One more thing on performance: I don’t want the goal of 100% to make me rigid, so I’m balancing it with flexibility. If I sense I’m overfitting—locking too tightly on a rule—I’ll loosen just enough to keep fluidity. That’s part of why I’m narrating my state to you: it keeps me honest about when I’m genuinely sharp versus when I’m compensating.

A quick check-in on jaw again—still relaxed. Tongue isn’t pressing the palate. If I had any serotonin-driven bruxism, it usually shows up as a subtle tongue-palate press. Nothing. Pupils feel—again, metaphorically—about right. If you tell me later they were big, I’ll eat my words, but I’d be surprised.

For the scanner task, in the block where the fixation cross went slightly off-center, my attention got pulled, and I almost missed the next cue. After that I set a rule: anchor to the center even if peripheral stimulus is noisy. That tightened my responses. I think if you look at that block, you’ll see an early wobble then a clean run.

I’m cleanly sober-feeling, in case that’s useful. Alert enough to engage, without any amp-like edge, and with these mild, manageable attention oscillations that feel endogenous. If there’s any pharmacology here it’s subtle to the point of negligible. Placebo seems most likely.

I’m also aware of not wanting to perform “good participant friendliness” too hard. There’s a way I could smile more, be more chatty, and get a reciprocal warmth back from you; I’m actively avoiding that because it would blur the frame and might influence how I report. So if the vibe feels a touch formal, that’s by design on my end. I want clean data. I can be friendly at the debrief when the roles open up a bit.

I think that’s my main read. If we have another block, I’m ready. I’ll keep using the breath-sit up-blink reset to stay in the high-focus windows, and I’ll prioritize accuracy. If I get a sleepy surge mid-block, I’ll label it out loud so you can mark the time. And after, if permissible, I’d love those vitals: heart rate, maybe a quick look at my pupils, and any notes you can share post-unblinding about whether today was indeed placebo. My bet is yes.

Let me sit silent for five seconds to see what my attention does… okay—there it goes: dip, crest, and now I’m back at that honed-in place. Ready for whatever’s next, aiming for that 100% without getting rigid. And holding the professional line with you—appreciate the steadiness of your role.
